title: How a Bipolar Diagnosis Became the Foundation for a Million-Dollar PR Empire
guestBio: Gabrielle Scout Maio is the founder and CEO of Scouts Agency and author of The Emotional Entrepreneur. Diagnosed with bipolar disorder at 14, she transformed her mental health journey into entrepreneurial fuel, building a PR empire that has booked over 5,000 podcast episodes and amplified hundreds of female voices.
excerpt: What if the thing you think disqualifies you from success is actually your greatest asset? Gabrielle Scout Maio proves that emotional intelligence trumps hustle every time, building a PR empire while redefining what powerful leadership looks like.
metaDescription: From bipolar diagnosis to PR empire: Gabrielle Scout Maio's journey proves emotional intelligence beats hustle in building authentic success.
keyTakeaways: Mental health challenges can become entrepreneurial superpowers when properly channeled and understood.
Emotional intelligence is more valuable than traditional hustle culture in building sustainable business success.
Being early to innovation is difficult but creates lasting competitive advantages in business.
Motherhood can actually accelerate business performance by forcing efficiency and clarity of priorities.
Authentic leadership that embraces vulnerability creates stronger teams and better business outcomes.
Women leaders naturally expand possibilities for everyone around them through their leadership style.
Your pain has purpose and your story has power when channeled into meaningful work.

pullQuotes.text: Emotional intelligence — not hustle — is the real secret to success
Your pain has purpose. Your story has power

timestamps.label: From Mental Health Diagnosis to Entrepreneurial Vision
Building Scouts Agency and the Podcast Revolution

content:
What if everything you've been told about your limitations is actually a lie? What if the diagnosis that was supposed to define your struggles became the foundation for your greatest success? In this powerful episode of The Manage Her, Aimee sits down with Gabrielle Scout Maio to explore one of the most compelling entrepreneurial journeys you'll ever hear.

At 14, Gabrielle received a bipolar diagnosis that came with a crushing prediction: she would struggle to hold a job. Instead of accepting this limitation, she discovered something revolutionary. Entrepreneurship wasn't just a career path for her—it was her superpower. Seven years after launching Scouts Agency in 2019, she's built a PR empire that has booked over 5,000 podcast episodes and amplified hundreds of female voices across the globe.

Transforming Pain into Purpose

Gabrielle's story challenges everything we think we know about success and mental health. Rather than hiding from her diagnosis or letting it define her limitations, she learned to harness the very traits that made her different. The emotional depth that comes with bipolar disorder became her greatest business asset, allowing her to connect with clients on a level that traditional PR agencies simply couldn't match.

This transformation didn't happen overnight. It required Gabrielle to completely reframe her relationship with her mental health, viewing it not as a weakness to overcome but as a strength to leverage. She discovered that the emotional intelligence required to navigate bipolar disorder translated directly into the kind of authentic leadership that builds lasting businesses.

The Birth of a PR Revolution

When Gabrielle launched Scouts Agency, she wasn't just starting another PR firm—she was pioneering an entirely new approach to public relations. She recognized early that podcast tours would become the future of PR, long before the industry caught on. Being early to this innovation wasn't easy. It required constant education of potential clients and the courage to stick with a vision when others couldn't see it yet.

Within just three months of launching, Gabrielle was able to quit her traditional job. But the real magic happened in how she built the business differently. Instead of following the typical agency playbook of grinding and hustling, she created a model based on genuine relationships, emotional intelligence, and authentic storytelling. This approach didn't just work—it revolutionized how her clients showed up in the world.

Motherhood as an Unexpected Business Accelerator

One of the most surprising insights from Gabrielle's journey is how motherhood actually improved her business performance. Conventional wisdom suggests that having a child will slow down or derail entrepreneurial momentum. Gabrielle's experience proves the opposite. Her best revenue year came when she was operating at just 30% capacity due to new motherhood.

The forced efficiency that comes with motherhood eliminated all the busy work that wasn't actually moving the needle. When you only have limited time and energy, you become laser-focused on what truly matters. The clarity that emerged from this constraint didn't just maintain her business—it accelerated it in ways that surprised everyone, including Gabrielle herself.

This revelation challenges the narrative that women must choose between serious ambition and motherhood. Instead, it suggests that the skills developed through motherhood—prioritization, emotional intelligence, efficiency, and the ability to see the bigger picture—are exactly the skills that create exceptional leaders.

Leading with Emotional Intelligence

Perhaps the most powerful aspect of Gabrielle's approach is her rejection of traditional hustle culture in favor of emotional intelligence. In a business world that often rewards those who can work the longest hours or push the hardest, she's proven that understanding emotions—both your own and others'—creates more sustainable and ultimately more profitable businesses.

This emotional approach extends to how she builds her team and company culture. Rather than extracting maximum productivity from her employees, she focuses on nurturing them to their next level of growth. This philosophy doesn't just create better work environments—it creates better business results. When people feel genuinely supported and understood, they bring their best work to everything they do.

Expanding Possibilities for Everyone

What emerges from Gabrielle's story is a broader truth about female leadership. Women leaders, when they embrace their authentic selves rather than trying to fit into traditional masculine leadership models, naturally expand possibilities for everyone around them. They create space for vulnerability, emotional intelligence, and different ways of thinking about success.

This expansion isn't just nice to have—it's a competitive advantage. In a world where authentic connection and genuine relationships matter more than ever, leaders who can create genuine human connections will consistently outperform those who rely solely on traditional business tactics.

Your Story Has Power

If you're sitting on a dream, carrying a story you think disqualifies you from success, or wondering if your struggles make you less capable of building something meaningful, Gabrielle's journey offers a different narrative. Your pain has purpose. Your story has power. The very things you think make you different or difficult might actually be your greatest business assets.

The key is learning to see your challenges through the lens of possibility rather than limitation. What looks like a barrier from one angle might be your unique competitive advantage from another. Gabrielle's success isn't despite her bipolar diagnosis—it's because she learned to channel the gifts that come with her particular brain chemistry into building something extraordinary.

This episode isn't just about one woman's remarkable journey from mental health struggles to business success. It's about reimagining what's possible when we stop trying to fit into other people's definitions of success and start building from our authentic selves. It's about recognizing that emotional intelligence, vulnerability, and different ways of thinking aren't weaknesses to overcome—they're strengths to leverage.

guestLinks.label: 📚 Get your copy of The Manage Her

quiz.title: What's Your Superpower Leadership Style?
quiz.description: Discover how your unique traits and experiences shape your leadership approach. Just like Gabrielle turned her bipolar diagnosis into a PR empire, your differences might be your greatest strengths.

questions.question: When facing a major setback or challenge, what's your natural response?

options.text: I dive deep into understanding what this experience is teaching me and how I can use it
I immediately start looking for new approaches or innovative solutions others haven't tried
I reach out to my network and have honest conversations about what I'm going through
I connect it back to my bigger why and figure out how this fits into my larger mission

questions.question: What energizes you most about leadership?

options.text: Helping people transform their struggles into strengths
Being first to market with game-changing ideas
Creating environments where people feel safe to be themselves
Building something that makes a lasting difference in the world

questions.question: How do you handle being different from other leaders in your industry?

options.text: I've learned that my differences are actually my competitive advantages
I use my unique perspective to spot opportunities others miss
I share my authentic story to help others feel less alone
I stay focused on my mission rather than trying to fit in

questions.question: When building your team, what matters most to you?

options.text: Finding people who understand that vulnerability creates strength
Attracting innovators who aren't afraid to challenge the status quo
Building genuine relationships based on trust and mutual respect
Aligning everyone around a shared sense of purpose and impact

questions.question: What's your relationship with traditional business advice?

options.text: I've learned to trust my emotional intelligence over generic formulas
I study it but often find myself creating entirely new approaches
I prefer learning from real conversations with other leaders
I filter everything through whether it aligns with my values and mission

questions.question: How do you view your past struggles or challenges?

options.text: They've given me depth and empathy that fuel my success today
They've taught me to think outside the box and find unconventional solutions
They've made me more authentic and able to connect with others
They've clarified my purpose and what I'm meant to contribute

results.title: The Emotional Alchemist
results.description: You have an incredible ability to transform difficult experiences into fuel for success. Your deep emotional intelligence allows you to connect authentically with others and see opportunities where others see obstacles. You don't just lead with your head—you lead with your whole heart, and that's what makes you unforgettable. This episode will show you how to harness your sensitivity as a competitive advantage in business.

results.title: The Strategic Pioneer
results.description: You're naturally drawn to innovation and have a gift for spotting trends before they become mainstream. You think differently and aren't afraid to take calculated risks that others might shy away from. Your leadership style combines vision with practical execution, making you someone others look to for direction. Gabrielle's story of being early to podcast PR will resonate deeply with your pioneering spirit.

results.title: The Authentic Connector
results.description: Your superpower lies in creating genuine relationships and building trust through vulnerability. You understand that real leadership isn't about having all the answers—it's about creating space for others to shine. You naturally expand possibilities for everyone around you through your inclusive approach. This episode will inspire you to lean even more into authenticity as your leadership edge.

results.title: The Purposeful Builder
results.description: You excel at turning your personal mission into meaningful work that impacts others. You understand that your story has power and you're not afraid to use it to create change. Your leadership style focuses on sustainable growth and helping others discover their own potential. Gabrielle's journey from diagnosis to million-dollar business will show you new ways to channel your purpose into profit.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Hi everyone and welcome back to The Manager, the podcast where we reframe the invisible work of women as powerful leadership. I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, author of The Manager, entrepreneur and mama of six. Today's guest is someone who embodies the art of turning pain into purpose and purpose into impact. 

Gabrielle Scout Maio is the founder and CEO of Scout's Agency, the go-to firm for entrepreneurs, authors, and thought leaders who want to amplify their message through the power of podcasts. But Scout's story didn't just start with success. It started with struggle. Before entrepreneurship, she was floundering, battling bipolar disorder, and searching for something that felt aligned.

Then in 2018, she discovered entrepreneurship and everything changed. Within three months of starting her business in 2019, she quit her job and never looked back. Since then, Scout's agency has booked over 5,000 podcast episodes, pioneered the concept of the podcast tour, and helped hundreds of voices become movements. She's also the author of The Emotional Entrepreneur, a collection of 25 emotional lessons for anyone building a business from the inside out.

Today we're talking about what happens when motherhood meets ambition, why being early in innovation is hard but worth it, and how emotional intelligence, not hustle, is the real secret to success. Welcome to the show, Scout. It's so nice to have you here today.

**Gabrielle:** Oh my gosh, I'm so honored. I've been looking forward to this. I'm really excited. Thank you.

**Aimee:** It's so fun. It's fun to have another mom entrepreneur. You've got littles, the little girl, toddler age like me.

**Gabrielle:** Singular, not plural. Just one. Which is the hardest one, actually. The number one is the - that one's boot camp. I hear mixed reviews. I hear that the first is the hardest. I hear the second is the hardest, but I do hear that once you hit three, it's just bring more and it doesn't matter.

**Aimee:** It is so true. That's what I honestly... yeah. When I went from one to two, I was scared. But two to three was like, okay, all right, we got this. And then once you're at three, you're just like - and then you don't already have enough hands to hold everyone's hand anyway. So if they can just hold each other's hands, let's go.

**Gabrielle:** Yeah, that's what I hear. But one's been amazing. It's been definitely an adjustment. A lot of things that I know we'll get into, especially when it comes to business, that you just don't know until you have a kid and you're living it.

**Aimee:** Oh, yeah. No, it all seems so easy when you're watching it from the outside and then you're in it and you're like, "Wow, this is 24/7 and it doesn't stop."

**Gabrielle:** I know. I remember being such a jerk to my friends who didn't have kids. I'd be like, "Why can't you just get a babysitter and come to dinner with me?" Looking back, I'm like, "Wow." There's so many things that once you have a kid that you realize are just very perspective altering for sure.

**Aimee:** Oh, yeah. It's big time. It's a huge perspective shift. The world isn't about you anymore. Now, it's about this new little person. And it's kind of a huge perspective shift. And as a woman in business, it changes a lot of things. 

But before we get into all of that, before you became the founder of one of the leading podcast agencies, you said you were floundering and navigating bipolar disorder. Could you take us back to that chapter? What did finding entrepreneurship in 2018 unlock for you?

**Gabrielle:** I usually start my story at the age of 14 when I had my first depressive episode. But if you go back into my childhood, there were very early signs of mental health issues around four to five. The Spark Notes version is I had my first depressive episode at 14, was put into therapy. But it wasn't really until I left for college that some bigger warning signs were coming up. I was losing touch with reality, experiencing paranoia, anxiety, depression, psychosis, catatonia, etc.

And so when I was diagnosed, my therapist and my psychiatrist said that I wouldn't be able to function in society. It would be hard for me to hold down a job and really be something of myself. And that was pretty true. I started jobs and I quit them. I dropped out of college God knows how many times and it was really hard for me to stand on my own two feet.

And then one day I was sitting with my friend at a coffee shop and I had just come back from a trip to New York with my family and I brought home this magazine, this indie magazine, and I looked at her and I said, "Why don't we start a magazine?" And she said, "Sure." And the plan was we were going to print it at Kinko's and we were going to take all the photos with Polaroids and we were just going to pass it out to our friends.

All of a sudden, an hour after we decided that, something in my brain just switched and I got the domain name, the Instagram handle. I started emailing printers. All of a sudden, I have five meetings with the best printers in Orange County. They're quoting me $10,000. I'm like, great. I have to do a Kickstarter. I do a Kickstarter. We raise the money. We have three issues. The third is in Barnes & Noble in newsstands across the country and Hailey's on the cover of our third magazine.

**Aimee:** No way.

**Gabrielle:** I was 22 and all of a sudden I was like, "Oh my god." I wasn't placed in the right lane. And when I found entrepreneurship, it worked for me for a couple of reasons. One, bipolar disorder, you have high highs and low lows. Two, I could always have my psychiatrist write me a note to get out of something that I didn't want to do because it felt too painful and too tough to see it through. So having responsibility was hard for me at that time.

But if you put all the responsibility on my shoulders, then I show up because I couldn't write - who was I going to write a sick note to Barnes & Noble? I have a purchase order. And so suddenly I found not only a framework that worked with my mind, but a framework that really made me excited and fulfilled. And that's when I said to myself, wow, there is a purpose and a path forward for me that is successful.

And it was also the start of me realizing how much power I had placed into other people's hands. Very similarly, they told me that I was too severe of a case to get off medication and get pregnant naturally. And so I was looking at surrogates and I was going to outsource, in my opinion, the most important experience and job I've ever had, which was growing my baby girl.

It's really been through many different ways. Entrepreneurship showed me that limitations that other people put on you aren't necessarily true. You just haven't found the right avenue for you. And so, ever since I found entrepreneurship, it's been 10, 15 years of me taking radical responsibility for myself, me choosing what I'm going to do in this lifetime and showing up. And entrepreneurship was the thing that gave me that vehicle and gave me the confidence to really do that.

**Aimee:** Heck yeah. Yeah. That's why I love it, too. I'm not even bipolar, but I get it. When you're in a manic episode, it's the perfect time to launch something.

**Gabrielle:** Oh my god, it's so good. And I'm like, "What's wrong? I'm a little manic and I'm just working a lot and being successful. There could be worse things that I'm doing."

**Aimee:** I get it. I totally get it. I'm like, "Oh, I could see how that would absolutely work." You need that extra energy because launching something is - you need that extra fuel to get something off the ground. But that's one of my favorite things to do.

**Gabrielle:** Oh my gosh. I mean, entrepreneurship is a high. There's no doubt about it. And listen, we pay equally for the high because the lows are really tough. But I think you're either built for it or you're not. And if you are built for it, once you find it, it's really hard to do anything else.

**Aimee:** Yes. Yes. I hear that so often from so many of my guests. If it's for you, there's really nothing else that you could do.

**Gabrielle:** Yes. There isn't. I say I'm stuck here. I could never work a job. It wouldn't work for me.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Yeah. Totally. My husband and I call ourselves unemployable.

**Gabrielle:** Oh, I am so unemployable. I don't even know what I would be like if I had a boss. I don't think it would be a very strange world. That's for sure.

**Aimee:** Different. Yeah. No, I know. It's no, thank you. You started your podcast agency in 2019, and within 3 months, you were able to quit your job. So, what do you think made Scout's agency take off so quickly?

**Gabrielle:** That's such a good question. By the time I started Scout's Agency, I had tried a bunch of other businesses that did not work in my early 20s. And I think I had something to prove to myself and to my family. I dropped out of college. I just really wanted to make a name for myself and I wanted to start a business that would actually make me money and support me. I wanted to prove to the world that I could do this. So there was a little bit of that and that goes a long way. That's a big motivator.

But when I started my podcast in 2018 with my sister, which we no longer do - we did it for 7 years. It was run on one of the top networks. We had every guest under the sun. It was the most incredible experience. I fell in love with the medium. And I was having the most beautiful in-depth concentrated conversations with women I never thought I had access to. I did not have a network. I did not have Instagram followers. My career was basically a question mark.

And all of a sudden, there's a place for me to talk to bestselling authors and entrepreneurs and reality TV stars and content creators. And that's something people don't realize about podcasting is how accessible it allows you to build a network. Really, people that you don't think you have access to, you can have access to if you have a podcast.

So, I was falling in love with the medium. And what I saw happening was that when I would publish these episodes, I would watch our community follow our guests online, buy their product, sign up for their course, buy their book. And that's when I realized that being a guest on a podcast was a form of PR. And not just any form of PR, but one that had such deep ROI because it was so human.

There's nowhere else in the social digital media landscape that you could go to and spend up to an hour with someone where they are speaking in a very intimate and vulnerable way. The way I'm talking with you now would be different if I was on stage talking to 2,000 people, right?

**Aimee:** Absolutely.

**Gabrielle:** And so people get to be a fly on the wall to very small intimate conversations and that helps them emotionally connect with the person and if they resonate and hear something in them that is going on in their life, they then are more - it's a stickier first impression and they're more prone to go follow them, support them, sign up for their business, etc.

And so I saw it happening in real time and when I say the story and I'm telling the origin story because it's going to get to why it became successful so quickly. When I say it back it sounds very polished. This was not as polished. I was like I'm going to try this. I saw something happening. I saw it working. I realized no one else was doing it and I was like let me try.

And I think that is one of the biggest misconceptions about entrepreneurship and business is that you have this super clear idea in the beginning and the first thing you start is the home run. This was my fifth thing that I started and it was just this willingness to try again and to be curious and to keep my eyes open for things that I felt I could fill in a market that I liked.

And so I found a market I liked. I found something that wasn't happening and I was like let's try. I had a media kit. I emailed a thousand women the first 24 hours. Gmail blocked my email. I opened up another email account. I had two email accounts, which was a total show operationally for a year. It was honestly this hunger. It was two things. It was a hunger and it was I found something that didn't exist.

And people ask, "Oh, well, did you know that - sounds like you got lucky finding something that didn't exist?" And it's like, "No, because it took me 5 years to find that thing." And so, keep your eyes open. See what you like. See what excites you.

And so in the beginning, I had something that wasn't being offered by pretty much anyone else. My prices were stupid low, so it was pretty much a no-brainer for people to work with me, right? And I hustled. I mean, a thousand pitch emails went out within 48 hours to work with me. Cold emails. Three months in, I was making just as much money as I was making working for my mom, which is the only day job I've ever had. I was employee number three in her startup. So, it wasn't even that buttoned up.

I think there one as an entrepreneur, you have to not be risk averse. And I wasn't risk aware. I was masochistic, I think, at that point. But I was also 27 and I didn't have kids and I was naive and I'd never done this before. And I think it was that perfect storm of I found something that didn't exist. I was hungry and I had a chip on my shoulder, something to prove. And I was in a position of naivety and not really having anyone that I was responsible for to make some pretty risky decisions in that first year.

And that's how we hit over $100,000 in our first year. And then we just doubled, tripled, quadrupled year after year. And it's been amazing. And now I look back at that girl and I'm like, "Wow, she really put herself out there." And I'm really grateful that she did.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Yeah. We do. You today can take risks that will benefit the you of the future.

**Gabrielle:** Yes. Yes. The risks I take now look a little different. I feel really grateful that I put myself every single day in situations that were highly uncomfortable. That first year I hired my first employee. I signed a lease on an office. I started growing my team. I went pretty fast. And it was hard. It was a hard year, but it was one of the most fulfilling years I think of my life.

**Aimee:** Yeah. I love the startup phase of anything. It's like being in love, that first year of dating somebody.

**Gabrielle:** Yeah. It really is. Everything is just perfect and you really haven't run into the cracks yet, usually, of seeing things that the pitfalls. The first year is just absolute magic.

**Aimee:** Yeah, it is. It was magic. So, that's why I think it worked. I think it was a perfect storm.

**Gabrielle:** Yes. I love it.

**Aimee:** So, you were one of the first to see podcast tours as the new PR. How has podcasting changed the landscape of marketing and thought leadership?

**Gabrielle:** Significantly. Especially when video started getting introduced because that's when it started bleeding into social media. And we spoke a little bit about this earlier. Seven years ago, podcasting, there was still an educational element that I had to tell people about. Not everybody was listening to them.

Now, today, a podcast is a brand extension at this point. Very similar to how brands have Instagram or TikToks or Substacks, well, now they're doing Substacks, they have podcasts. And so, it has not just become - I think in the beginning it was the democratization of radio, right? People could just have their own talk shows and it was influencer based or news-based.

And now we're really seeing it move into the business space. So not only are thought leaders using podcasts as a way to go deep with their community. It's a way to develop content that isn't 90 seconds but actually provides a lot of depth. It's also a place where your community can come and hear a lot more about you. Typically thought leaders have a message that has more depth. They typically have books, right, like you. They're authors.

So authors and podcasting goes very hand in hand because it's a medium that offers more long form ways to communicate. So we're seeing that happen where podcasting is becoming a marketing and business brand strategy for thought leaders and businesses. And then with the introduction to video, it's also - I mean sometimes I tell my clients don't even worry about the downloads. Just have a podcast for marketing and content at some point too.

So, it's really gone away from, in my opinion, the purest version of what a podcast is, which was audio only. And it's become a marketing machine for thought leaders and businesses now, too, who are placing executives as the thought leader representative of that company. It really is the human first way to market your business. That's why it lends itself to thought leadership so well. That's why podcast content does so good on social media.

So, it's been really cool to see that evolution from this more casual audio only medium to now we're talking it's a business strategy for thought leaders and brands that want to develop thought leadership within their businesses.

**Aimee:** Yeah. It's like you and I were saying it's not just a podcast now. It's like a TV show because you're really in full production.

**Gabrielle:** Oh, yeah. And there's so many layers to this. This is happening right now, but I'm taking behind the scenes photos that I'm going to post on Instagram, right? So then my community is going to see me in the interviewee seat as an expert, which is going to create thought leadership for my existing community. Even if they never listen to this podcast, I can put it in my newsletter at Scout's Agency that's going to catapult me even further into thought leadership and expertise. I'm going to put a social media reel up.

When you really start thinking about it as that, there are 90% of people on the internet that are going to see you on the podcast that are never going to listen to the podcast, but then the 10% that do are the ultimate fans converting, really deep part of your client base or community.

So, I really urge people to think of podcasts less of just a content channel and much more of a show, a production, and a marketing vehicle for so much of your business if you use it properly.

**Aimee:** Yeah, it really is nice. I feel like it takes the ideas that were in my book and allows me - because there's so much meat on the bone. You can only put so much in a book and then it's like, no, let's expand this idea. Let's really get in and talk about how the invisible work that women do as leadership, and I can do that now with all of these amazing women that are thought leaders in their own right and leaders in their own right. And it's so cool to be able to expand the idea and the concepts of the manager in this very expansive way.

**Gabrielle:** Yeah. The podcast is so expansive. It's such a long format. It's so intimate, and you're meeting them. You're bringing other voices in too. The amount of women that you're going to network with at the same time. That's why it's my favorite because I'm like find me another medium that scratches the itch of I get to go deep and I get to, as you said, there's so much more meat that I really want to dive into and so we could have really impactful conversations that you and I leave feeling lit up about and then what it does to your community, my community, your business, my business, our network. It's the gift that keeps on giving.

**Aimee:** It really is. There's so much synergy. I felt like there's so much synergy. When I was exploring the concept, I'm like, "Do I just want to do solo?" And I'm like, "No, I don't want to do solo." There's so much synergy in the guest, especially for what you're talking about.

**Gabrielle:** Yeah. Yeah. So much synergy there because you're talking about two businesses now. You're talking about two thought leaders in the same room, two high-level people that have done cool stuff having conversations about doing cool stuff. And when it comes to the invisible work and stuff that women don't talk about behind the scenes. Like potty training.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Oh my god. I was just going to curse again. You're making me angry. I told Scout just bring her little girl over to my house. I'll potty train her. I'm getting really good at this. Oh my god. I might - you might - you can hear a knock on the door. I'm going to throw her and run away.

**Gabrielle:** Fine. Totally. We'll keep her for a weekend. We'll have her potty trained. No big deal.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Exactly. You give me hope.

**Gabrielle:** Yeah. No, honestly, it's all about confidence. It really is.

**Aimee:** I don't have it. Be confident.

**Gabrielle:** I don't have the confidence.

**Aimee:** No, but you don't. When it's your first time, I swear the more kids I potty trained, the more confident I got. And I'm like, oh, okay. Today's the day we're going to put the underpants on. This is it. Well, he's going to pee in the underpants a few times. But, as I said, there's always more pee than poo. So, it's, you had to learn on pee.

**Gabrielle:** I'm a one and done kind of girl, kind of mom. So, this is my only chance. So, maybe I should develop a little bit of confidence so I don't screw this one up.

**Aimee:** Oh, you've got this. It's not as hard as it sounds. I think that's good. Yeah. I think if you overthink it, you can really over complicate it. But really, the idea is they can really do this. It's just a matter of giving them the confidence. If you believe they can do it, they'll believe they can do it.

**Gabrielle:** Yeah. It's so true. It was so cute. I was filming her and I said, "Do you want to go in the toilet? Do you want to go pee pee in the toilet?" And she looks at me and she goes, "Not yet. When I'm big or older?" "Not yet." And I'm like, "Okay, well, will you be big or older tomorrow?" "No, not yet."

**Aimee:** Okay. You're like, "Okay, we'll try again later."

**Gabrielle:** Yeah. No rush. No rush.

**Aimee:** But usually, I mean, they'll tell you when they're ready, too. Most of them. I think she's in preschool. She sees other kids.

**Gabrielle:** Oh, yeah. No.
